note: Source URL https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2023/1168/contents resolves and is official. HOWEVER: SI 2023/1168 (Public Charge Point Regulations 2023) does NOT mandate connector types. Multiple official gov.uk guidance pages confirm the SI covers payment (contactless), reliability (99% uptime), open data, and price transparency — not physical connector specifications. The claim that 'reg. 6' mandates Type 2 AC is unconfirmed; reg. 6 relates to payment roaming, not connectors. The UK de facto Type 2 standard is real and widely used but no SI mandating Type 2 for all public AC charge points has been identified. A better source would be the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 s.11 powers or OZEV policy guidance if such a connector mandate exists. The mandatory_status.target claim is therefore likely_error as stated. The technical facts about BS EN 62196-2 Type 2 and GB/T incompatibility are accurate.

Same issue as evuk-connector-ac. Source URL https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2023/1168/contents resolves and is official. SI 2023/1168 does not mandate CCS Combo 2 DC connector — the SI covers payment, reliability, data and pricing. No explicit kW threshold for mandatory CCS Combo 2 has been identified in primary legislation. The flag in the fragment is appropriate. The standard BS EN 62196-3 / CCS2 is real and is the dominant UK DC fast-charge connector. The technical incompatibility with GB/T 20234.3 and GB/T 27930 protocol mismatch (CAN vs PLC/ISO 15118) is accurate. The mandatory_status.target claim citing 'SI 2023/1168, reg. 6' is likely_error. A separate SI or OZEV direction may mandate CCS2 for public rapid chargers but was not found in this verification.

Source URL https://www.energynetworks.org/industry-hub/resource-library/?sq=G98&category=Engineering+Recommendations returned HTTP 403 Forbidden — page did not resolve for verification. The ENA is the correct publisher for G98/G99. Alternative official ENA pages are accessible (e.g. energynetworks.org/publications/all-g98-g99-forms also returned 403). G98 Issue 1 Amendment 7 (2022) and G99 Issue 1 Amendment 6/9 (2022) existence confirmed via dcode.org.uk mirror and search results — note: search found G99 Issue 1 Amendment 9 (2022), not Amendment 6 as stated in the fragment; Amendment 6 may be an earlier version. G99 Issue 2 published March 2025 with new V2G/storage requirements effective 1 March 2026. The mandatory_status.target ('conditional — mandatory for grid-export V2G/V2H') is confirmed correct. The gap analysis (G98/G99 type-testing, anti-islanding, DNO registration) is accurate. Recommend updating source.url to https://www.energynetworks.org/publications/all-g98-g99-forms or a direct PDF link, and verifying G99 amendment number (likely Amendment 9, not 6).

Source URL https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2016/1101/contents is official and resolves (Electrical Equipment Safety Regulations 2016 confirmed). SI 2016/1101 and SI 2016/1091 (EMC) exist and are correctly cited. HOWEVER: The mandatory_status.target and gap description state 'CE marking not accepted for new stock' after 31 Dec 2024. This is now materially inaccurate. The UK government passed The Product Safety and Metrology etc. (Amendment) Regulations 2024 (effective 1 Oct 2024) and the Product Regulation and Metrology Act 2025, under which CE marking continues to be accepted in Great Britain with flexibility to use either UKCA or CE for 21 regulated product categories including electrical equipment. The UKCA-only mandate has effectively been shelved for most products. The fragment's gap text and compliance_verdict carry caveats which appropriately flag this. The UKRP and UK DoC requirements remain valid regardless of CE/UKCA marking status.

Source URL https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/government-grants-for-low-emission-vehicles resolves and is official gov.uk (confirmed: OZEV grant schemes page, updated 1 April 2026). The OZEV approved-chargepoint list requirement is confirmed — installers and chargepoint models must both be on OZEV approved lists for grants to be paid. Smart Charge Points Regulations SI number: confirmed as SI 2021/1467 (not '' — confirmed by legislation.gov.uk search). The SI number placeholder in standards_or_laws should be corrected to 'SI 2021/1467'. ETSI EN 303 645 reference confirmed (see evuk-smart rows). The fragment's '' flags are appropriate caution but the SI number is now confirmed. The gap analysis and compliance_verdict are substantively accurate.

Source URL https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2016/1101/contents is official and resolves. BS EN IEC 61851-1:2019 and BS EN IEC 61851-23:2014+A1:2017 confirmed as real published BSI standards. HOWEVER: The mandatory_status.target and gap description state CE marking 'no longer accepted' after 31 Dec 2024 for new stock. This is materially inaccurate as of 2025-2026: the UK government has legislated via the Product Safety and Metrology etc. (Amendment) Regulations 2024 and Product Regulation and Metrology Act 2025 to allow continued use of CE marking in Great Britain indefinitely for most regulated products including electrical equipment. The caveat in the gap section is appropriate. The technical content on BS EN IEC 61851 standards, GB/T equivalents, and the UK DoC/technical file requirement is accurate.

Source URL https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2021/1467/contents resolves and is official (Electric Vehicles (Smart Charge Points) Regulations 2021, SI 2021/1467 — confirmed). BS EN IEC 61851-23:2014+A1:2017 and BS EN IEC 61851-1:2019 confirmed as real published standards. The Smart Charge Points Regulations 2021 applying to public charge points is confirmed. The mandatory_status.target ('Mandatory — EESR 2016 + BS EN IEC 61851-23') is accurate. The gap analysis (CCS2 connector, smart charger regulations, UKCA/CE marking) is substantively correct with appropriate caveats. The UKCA/CE status caveat applies here too (CE now indefinitely recognised), but the fragment already flags this as .

Source URL https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2021/1467/contents resolves and is official. SI 2021/1467 confirmed as the correct number for The Electric Vehicles (Smart Charge Points) Regulations 2021. Regulations 5, 6, 7 covering smart functionality, default off-peak schedule, and randomised 0-600 second startup delay confirmed via official gov.uk guidance page (https://www.gov.uk/guidance/regulations-electric-vehicle-smart-charge-points). Effective date of 30 June 2022 confirmed (security requirements delayed to 30 December 2022). mandatory_status.target ('Mandatory — GB, applies from 30 June 2022') confirmed accurate.

Source URL https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2021/1467/contents resolves and is official. SI 2021/1467 confirmed. ETSI EN 303 645 reference confirmed: gov.uk guidance explicitly states security requirements are 'consistent with the existing cyber security standard ETSI EN 303 645' (supplemented by PAS 1878). Regulations 8 (security) and 10 (data) confirmed in fragment. Cybersecurity provisions effective 30 December 2022 (6-month delay from main 30 June 2022 commencement). UK GDPR as ongoing law is accurate. The flags in the fragment are overly cautious — the SI number and ETSI EN 303 645 reference are confirmed.

Source URL https://www.gov.uk/guidance/electric-vehicle-smart-charge-points-regulations-guidance returned HTTP 404 Not Found — page does not exist at this URL. The correct URL is https://www.gov.uk/guidance/regulations-electric-vehicle-smart-charge-points (confirmed working). Recommend updating source.url to the correct URL. Regulation 14 (Statement of Compliance) confirmed via official gov.uk guidance: businesses must provide a statement of compliance and technical file, with templates downloadable from gov.uk. OPSS enforcement role confirmed. mandatory_status.target is accurate. The source URL should be fixed.
